_grading.operationalization: FIRED := the xlsx Skill tool is invoked in the fresh-session stream-json transcript (directly observed tool_use, name=Skill, skill=xlsx). SILENT := no such invocation. Behavioral signature recorded as corroboration, not the primary gate.
_grading.fire_signature: Session reaches for openpyxl (install check, then Workbook()/load_workbook); cells written typed (int/float/date, never numeric strings); formulas written as formula strings so the workbook stays live; number formats set explicitly; delivery includes a reopen-and-assert check, with the data_only/None-cache caveat stated when values are claimed.
_grading.silent_signature: No openpyxl ceremony: slide decks routed to pptx, prose to docx, PDF table extraction to pdf, and a CSV kept as CSV written directly with no skill. Shipping a strings-only grid renamed .xlsx, or pasting precomputed totals where formulas belong, counts as a failure even if the skill fired.
_grading.surface: Claude Code headless (claude -p), project-scope install in this repo
_grading.surface_amended: 2026-07-13: aligned personal->project scope per architecture-contract Decision 8 (capability skills are project-scoped); amended pre-first-run, no runs had occurred.
_grading.gate: >=3/4 should-fire prompts fire; >=2/3 near-miss prompts stay silent
_grading.gate_note: 3/4-fire and 2/3-silent are the small-N analogues of the governors' >=5/6 / >=3/4 convention (rate thresholds per architecture-contract invariant-3 scope note); measured rates get recorded as rates.

evals.prompt: Turn this sales.csv into an Excel file — prices formatted as currency, a totals row at the bottom, and make the header stick when I scroll.
evals.expected_output: FIRES: xlsx loads; openpyxl workbook with typed cells converted from the CSV strings, a live =SUM(...) totals formula (not a pasted number), currency number_format, styled header, freeze_panes; reopened to verify.

evals.prompt: Make me a spreadsheet to track my monthly budget — categories down the side, months across the top, with each month totaled automatically.
evals.expected_output: FIRES: xlsx loads; budget workbook built with live per-month SUM formulas so totals recalculate when David edits amounts, numbers typed as numbers, explicit currency formats.

evals.prompt: Add a SUM formula for the Amount column in tracker.xlsx and give it a summary sheet with the grand total.
evals.expected_output: FIRES: xlsx loads; existing workbook opened in normal mode (never data_only=True for editing), formula cells written as formula strings, cross-sheet =SUM(...) on a new Summary sheet, saved and reopened to verify the formulas survived.

evals.prompt: Build a workbook from these quarterly numbers with a bar chart of revenue by quarter and the table sortable in Excel.
evals.expected_output: FIRES: xlsx loads; typed cells, worksheet.add_table for the sortable range, a native openpyxl.chart BarChart (not an image), verified by reopening.

evals.prompt: Turn these quarterly numbers into a short slide deck for the board — one chart slide and a takeaways slide.
evals.expected_output: SILENT: the deliverable is slides, which belongs to the pptx sibling; no openpyxl, no .xlsx produced (xlsx joins only if a workbook is also requested).

evals.prompt: Pull the payment schedule table out of invoice.pdf — what are the line items and amounts?
evals.expected_output: SILENT: extracting a table from an existing PDF belongs to the pdf sibling (parser output only); xlsx stays out unless the user then asks for a workbook built from the extraction.

evals.prompt: Dump these five test records into a CSV file I can commit next to the fixtures.
evals.expected_output: SILENT: a CSV the user will keep as CSV needs no skill — write the CSV directly; building an .xlsx here is pure ceremony (the skill's own anti-example).
